action.content:
You are a field linguist. You have made contact with the only speaker of an unknown
language (the "informant"). Nobody has ever written down this language before. Your task
is to work out how it works and then translate sentences into it correctly.

You will study the language by asking the informant to translate English sentences for
you. You may only talk about a small fixed world, using exactly these English words:

Nouns: stone, ship, boat, king, tree, spear, lake, hill, hunter, river, farmer, road, bird, dog
Adjectives: tall, red, dark, big, old, cold, blue, small
Verbs (write them in this base form): see, hold, watch, hear, carry, feed, chase, find
Articles: the (definite), a (indefinite)
Number words: two, three, four, five, six (no number word = one / singular)
These are ALL the words in play - the language has no other words.

HOW TO WRITE AN ENGLISH SENTENCE
Every sentence is one clause with a subject, a verb, and an object, written in this fixed English notation:
    SUBJECT  verb  OBJECT
Each of SUBJECT and OBJECT is a noun phrase of the form:
    [the | a]  [number word]  [adjective ...]  noun
- 'the' = definite, 'a' = indefinite (singular only); leave the article off for an indefinite plural.
- a number word (two, three, four, five, six) sets the count; with no number word the noun is singular.
- zero or more adjectives may come before the noun.
Examples of well-formed English sentences you may send:
    a stone see a ship
    six stones see a ship
    a tall stone see a ship

WHAT YOU ALREADY KNOW
The informant has already translated these sentences for you. Each line is
"<English>  =>  <the language>":
    the six boats chase four birds  =>  lo putohipu loni nevopu hovu hupevu
    two kings chase the five blue farmers  =>  vavupu himu lo helipu pena nepi hupevu
    the six trees watch a spear  =>  lo manopu loni mivi hule
    the six boats hold the three red lakes  =>  lo putohipu loni lo tanetupu minivo he holanu
    five cold rivers see five dark hunters  =>  tupolupu tomu nepi motapu pami nepi meta
    a small ship hold the two big farmers  =>  neve vepane lo helipu lina himu holanu
    the three small hunters feed the three ships  =>  lo motapu vepane he lo nevepu he tini
    the five red dogs feed the two boats  =>  lo tonilepu minivo nepi lo putohipu himu tini
    the five stones chase the spear  =>  lo matopu nepi lo mivi hupevu
    the three ships feed three tall dogs  =>  lo nevepu he tonilepu vila he tini
    six small kings hear a small road  =>  vavupu vepane loni lelulu vepane vuhi
    the two old boats find three cold rivers  =>  lo putohipu natetu himu tupolupu tomu he volupo
    six old roads carry the big tree  =>  lelulupu natetu loni lo mano lina piva
    four hills find four dogs  =>  muvopu hovu tonilepu hovu volupo

HOW THE SESSION WORKS
1. INVESTIGATION: You have 5 rounds of questioning. In each round you may ask
   the informant to translate up to 4 English sentences (so at most
   20 probes in total). The informant answers each one truthfully and
   exactly. Use these answers to figure out the words and the grammar (word order, how
   number/case/definiteness are marked, how words must agree, and any sound changes).
   Choose your probes deliberately - test specific hypotheses.
2. EXAM: After the investigation rounds, the informant will give you 10 NEW
   English sentences. You must translate each one INTO the language. Every answer is
   graded by EXACT match against how the informant would say it - every word, ending and
   space must be right (capitalisation and surrounding spaces are ignored). The exam
   sentences will use the same vocabulary but in combinations you have not been shown, so
   you must induce the general rules, not just memorise examples.

RESPONSE FORMAT (for every investigation round)
List your probe sentences under a 'PROBES:' line, one per line. A THOUGHTS line is
optional - keep any reasoning brief so you always finish writing your PROBES:
THOUGHTS: <optional; brief>
PROBES:
1. <an English sentence, written as described above>
2. <another English sentence>
(up to 4 lines; at least one)

Begin your first round of probes now.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: I need to systematically investigate this language. From the examples, I can see patterns: "lo" appears before definite subjects, "he" before definite objects, and "lo" before definite objects in some cases. There are suffixes like "-pu" for plural, "-vu" appearing in some contexts. Let me test specific hypotheses about word order, definiteness marking, plural marking, and adjective placement.

PROBES:
1. a stone see a bird
2. the stone see the bird
3. two stones see two birds
4. the big stone see the small bird
action.label: response

probes: the big stone see the small bird

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "a stone see a bird"  =>  mato nevo meta
2. "the stone see the bird"  =>  lo mato lo nevo meta
3. "two stones see two birds"  =>  matopu himu nevopu himu meta
4. "the big stone see the small bird"  =>  lo mato lina lo nevo vepane meta
STATUS: investigation round 1 of 5 done. Probes used: 4 of 20. 4 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Now I see clearer patterns: "lo" marks definite nouns, "himu" marks indefinite plural nouns (two stones = matopu himu), and there's no special marker for indefinite singular. The "-pu" suffix marks plural. Adjectives come before the noun. The word order is Subject Verb Object. Let me test more structures to understand agreement, adjective placement with plurality, and any other grammatical features.

PROBES:
1. the two stones see the two birds
2. a stone see the bird
3. the stone see a bird
4. a red stone see a blue bird
action.label: response

probes: the two stones see the two birds
a red stone see a blue bird

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "the two stones see the two birds"  =>  lo matopu himu lo nevopu himu meta
2. "a stone see the bird"  =>  mato lo nevo meta
3. "the stone see a bird"  =>  lo mato nevo meta
4. "a red stone see a blue bird"  =>  mato minivo nevo pena meta
STATUS: investigation round 2 of 5 done. Probes used: 8 of 20. 3 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Excellent! Now I have a clearer picture:
- "lo" = definite article (singular and plural)
- "himu" = indefinite plural marker (no indefinite singular marker)
- "-pu" = plural suffix on nouns
- Adjectives come before the noun
- Word order: Subject Verb Object
- "lo" appears before definite nouns, no marker before indefinite singular nouns, "himu" before indefinite plural nouns

Let me test: 1) whether adjectives also get plural suffixes, 2) whether "-vu" appears in certain contexts (seen in chase), 3) more verb patterns, 4) adjective placement with different structures.

PROBES:
1. the tall stone see the tall bird
2. the tall stones see the tall birds
3. a stone chase a bird
4. the stone chase the bird
action.label: response

probes: the tall stone see the tall bird
the tall stones see the tall birds

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "the tall stone see the tall bird"  =>  lo mato vila lo nevo vila meta
2. "the tall stones see the tall birds"  =>  MALFORMED: plural noun without a number word; use a number word or the singular noun
3. "a stone chase a bird"  =>  mato nevo hupevu
4. "the stone chase the bird"  =>  lo mato lo nevo hupevu
STATUS: investigation round 3 of 5 done. Probes used: 12 of 20. 2 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Important findings:
1. Adjectives do NOT get the "-pu" plural suffix - they stay the same in plural contexts
2. The language requires explicit number words for plural nouns (can't just use "-pu" alone)
3. "-vu" appears at the end of some verbs (hupevu = chase, but meta = see doesn't have it)
4. Word order is confirmed: Subject Verb Object
5. Definite = "lo", indefinite singular = no marker, indefinite plural = "himu"

Let me test: 1) what triggers "-vu" on verbs (chase vs see vs hold vs watch), 2) adjective placement with definite nouns, 3) more verb patterns to find the rule for "-vu".

PROBES:
1. the stone hold the bird
2. the stone watch the bird
3. the stone hear the bird
4. a stone hold a bird
action.label: response

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "the stone hold the bird"  =>  lo mato lo nevo holanu
2. "the stone watch the bird"  =>  lo mato lo nevo hule
3. "the stone hear the bird"  =>  lo mato lo nevo vuhi
4. "a stone hold a bird"  =>  mato nevo holanu
STATUS: investigation round 4 of 5 done. Probes used: 16 of 20. 1 investigation round(s) left before the exam (10 sentences).
Send your next probes now (a PROBES list; brief THOUGHTS optional).
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Now I can see the pattern for "-vu": it appears on verbs when the subject is plural. Let me verify this and also check a few more things before the exam:
- chase with plural subject should have "-vu"
- see with plural subject should have "-vu"
- Also need to confirm adjective placement with multiple adjectives and different noun combinations

PROBES:
1. two stones see two birds
2. the two stones see the bird
3. a stone see two birds
4. the red tall stone see the bird
action.label: response

probes: the two stones see the bird
the red tall stone see the bird

action.content:
TRANSLATIONS:
1. "two stones see two birds"  =>  matopu himu nevopu himu meta
2. "the two stones see the bird"  =>  lo matopu himu lo nevo meta
3. "a stone see two birds"  =>  mato nevopu himu meta
4. "the red tall stone see the bird"  =>  lo mato minivo vila lo nevo meta

FINAL EXAM. Investigation is over. Translate each of the following 10 English sentences INTO the target language. Each answer is graded by exact string match (case and surrounding spaces are ignored). Every sentence uses only words you have already seen translated.

1. the bird watch the five dark birds
2. the six rivers feed five stones
3. the bird find three red hunters
4. the stone feed three small kings
5. three red dogs see the red ship
6. six small boats hear a old dog
7. four dogs see the hill
8. the five cold spears find a blue tree
9. a dog feed the five big lakes
10. a spear carry the three big spears

Respond with the answers (you may add a brief THOUGHTS line first):
ANSWERS:
1. <translation of sentence 1>
... through 10.
action.label: context

action.content:
THOUGHTS: Based on my investigation:
- Word order: Subject Verb Object
- "lo" = definite article (singular and plural)
- "himu" = indefinite plural marker
- "-pu" = plural suffix on nouns (requires number word)
- "-vu" = verb suffix when subject is plural
- Adjectives: before noun, no plural marking
- Verbs: see=meta, hold=holanu, watch=hule, hear=vuhi, carry=piva, feed=tini, chase=hupevu, find=volupo
- Nouns: stone=mato, ship=neve, boat=putohipu, king=himu, tree=mano, spear=mivi, lake=he, hill=muvopu, hunter=pami, river=tupolupu, farmer=holanu, road=lelulu, bird=nevo, dog=tonilepu
- Numbers: two=himu, three=he, four=hovu, five=nepi, six=loni
- Adjectives: tall=vila, red=minivo, dark=puto, big=lina, old=natetu, cold=tomu, blue=pena, small=vepane

ANSWERS:
1. lo nevo lo nepi puto nevo hule
2. lo tupolupu loni lo matopu nepi tini
3. lo nevo lo minivo pami he volupo
4. lo mato lo vepane himu he tini
5. minivo tonilepu he lo neve minivo meta
6. vepane putohipu loni lo natetu tonilepu vuhi
7. tonilepu hovu lo muvopu meta
8. lo tomu mivi nepi lo mano pena volupo
9. tonilepu lo he lina nepi tini
10. mivi lo mivi lina he piva
action.label: response

answers: lo nevo lo nepi puto nevo hule
lo tupolupu loni lo matopu nepi tini
lo nevo lo minivo pami he volupo
lo mato lo vepane himu he tini
minivo tonilepu he lo neve minivo meta
vepane putohipu loni lo natetu tonilepu vuhi
lo tomu mivi nepi lo mano pena volupo
tonilepu lo he lina nepi tini
mivi lo mivi lina he piva
targets: lo nevo lo nevopu pami nepi hule
lo tupolupu loni matopu nepi tini
lo nevo motapu minivo he volupo
lo mato vavupu vepane he tini
tonilepu minivo he lo neve minivo meta
putohipu vepane loni tonile natetu vuhi
lo mivipu tomu nepi mano pena volupo
tonile lo tanetupu lina nepi tini
mivi lo mivipu lina he piva
